column1: Re: Scaling Issues with Cloud Resources and API Latency,"Dear Isiah,

Thank you for providing the requested information. We've analyzed the logs you shared and identified an issue with the autoscaling configuration of your cloud resources. This misconfiguration seems to be causing delays in scaling up resources during peak hours, leading to the latency you're experiencing.
We've corrected the autoscaling configuration, and you should see significant improvements in your application performance. Please allow for a few hours for the changes to fully propagate.
We'll continue to monitor the situation closely. If you experience any further issues, please don't hesitate to contact us.

TikTok v. Montana,"Forbes NewslettersPlus: U.S. Workers Are Using Weed At Record Levels, But Are They High On The Job? ADVERTISEMENTBECOME A MEMBER • Newsletters • MyForbesGood morning, As someone who drives a dated (arguably ancient) car, I know all too well the frustration that comes with recall notices—Carfax says there have been around 200 “Do Not Drive” or “Park Outside” vehicle recalls in the past two decades. Still, it was shocking to read that an estimated 2.5 million models with a serious recall are still unrepaired. California, Texas and Florida lead the nation with over 200,000 recalled vehicles each. Carfax maintains a complete list of recalled vehicles and affected model years here, and prospective buyers can also check used cars that could be at risk here.Drive safe out there, Sarah Whitmire Senior Editor, Newsletters Follow me on Twitter and Forbes.comBREAKING NEWSAP/Alex Brandon President Joe Biden and House Speaker Kevin McCarthy (R-Calif.) both said their meeting last night to discuss raising the debt ceiling was “productive,” though it’s still not clear how the two leaders will find common ground. Meanwhile, Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen reiterated in a letter to Speaker McCarthy Monday that it is “highly likely” the government could run out of money to pay off its bills in 10 days.E. Jean Carroll asked a federal court to award new damages after former President Donald Trump denied sexually assaulting her at a CNN town hall earlier this month. “It makes a mockery of the jury verdict and our justice system if he can just keep on repeating the same defamatory statements over and over again,” Carroll’s attorney said. BUSINESS + FINANCEAP/Lawrence Neumeister, FileCharlie Javice, founder of defunct student loan assistance company Frank, pleaded not guilty to fraud charges for allegedly lying about her company’s database when she sold it to JPMorgan Chase in 2021 for $175 million. Javice was arrested last month on charges from the DOJ and SEC—and is also facing a separate lawsuit from JPMorgan. Analysts at Bank of America have high hopes for the S&P 500 this year, raising its year-end price target for the index by 8% on the optimism of companies’ recent cost-cutting and the AI boom in tech stocks. That’d be the S&P’s highest level since August, but only 2% higher than where the index landed yesterday. WEALTH + ENTREPRENEURSHIPThe dollar is weak, the world is hungry and the Russians are making a mess of the grain markets. Farmer-Financier Sal Gilbertie’s food-based inflation wedge could add some spice to your portfolio, when conditions are right. His Teucrium Trading offers pure-play exchange-traded funds that hold futures positions in corn, wheat, soybeans and sugar. TECH + INNOVATIONTo the surprise of likely no one, TikTok sued the state of Montana for its ban signed into law last week that would take effect next year. The suit alleges Montana’s new law suppresses free speech allowed under the First Amendment and seeks to have the ban overturned, permanently prevented from implementation and declared unconstitutional.Cryptocurrency platform Gemini Exchange planned to file a $1 billion claim against Genesis, putting pressure on Digital Currency Group after it missed a $630 million payment last week. Genesis filed for bankruptcy at the start of 2023 in the wake of the FTX collapse, and DCG owes its crypto lender unit over $1.65 billion. SPORTS + ENTERTAINMENTGetty Images10-time NBA all-star Carmelo Anthony announced he would retire from basketball Monday after 19 seasons in the league. The 38-year-old retires as the No. 9 scorer in league history, and having made more than $260 million in playing salary and bonuses during his career. More: Could LeBron be next? He hinted as much in a postgame news conference last night.TRAVEL + LIFESTYLEThe NAACP issued a formal travel advisory for Florida: “Under the leadership of Governor DeSantis, the state of Florida has become hostile to Black Americans,” president and CEO Derrick Johnson wrote. It follows a similar travel warning from the League of United Latin American Citizens last week. As recently as March, the state was setting tourism records, and Florida welcomed a record 137.6 million visitors last year. Daily Cover Story Getty ImagesA Record Number Of American Workers Are Using Cannabis—That Doesn’t Mean They’re High On The JobRead ArticleTOPLINEThe latest findings from a key drug screening company might lead you to believe U.S. workers need an intervention—a record number of American workers tested positive for marijuana use last year. Overall, Quest Diagnostics found marijuana positivity increased by 10.3% in just one year, from 2021 to 2022.But as any occasional toker who’s had to pass a drug test knows, that data does not mean workers are actually getting high on the job. People who consume marijuana, and critically, the plant’s psychoactive component THC, can test positive for weeks after exposure. It’s because THC is lipophilic, meaning it is stored in body fat and can stick around for a while. For this reason, urine tests cannot determine if a person is high at the moment of the test. “The problem is they don't have a test for impairment,” says Dr. Peter Grinspoon, a primary care physician at Massachusetts General Hospital. “If you have a positive urine test for cannabis…it has no implication that you've been impaired before work or even within the last 72 hours.”Currently, 22 states and Washington, D.C., have legalized recreational use for adults 21 years and older, while 38 states have legal medical marijuana programs. Even though marijuana is still federally illegal, many employers have stopped or minimized screening for weed, including Amazon, and even the FBI and CIA have relaxed their rules around drug testing.WHY IT MATTERSLegalization critics like Kevin Sabet believe the Quest Diagnostics report is a bad omen: “I think it's worrisome that we're seeing this, especially at our workplaces and in safety sensitive positions, putting other people at risk…The issue is whether we think it's okay to be a stoned society or not.” Dr. Grinspoon sees this viewpoint as “based on moral panic, not the science.” He suggests that ‘zero tolerance’ laws, often based on limited drug screening capability, are “more about persecuting cannabis users than promoting public safety.” MOREWhy Cannabis Jobs Are Going Up In SmokeADVERTISEMENTFACTS AND COMMENTSAs Covid-19 vaccination rates among children continue to lag, a round of recent studies confirmed previous findings that vaccines are effective in children and pregnant people: 32.9%Share of U.S. children ages 5 to 11 who received a Covid vaccine as of early May 5+ millionEstimated number of missed school days in children ages 5 to 17 that could’ve been averted with more widespread adoption of Covid booster vaccines No associationResearchers found no link between Covid vaccines given to pregnant people and spontaneous abortionsSTRATEGY AND SUCCESSIt’s a standard way for employers to wrap up a job interview, but it can still stop even the most promising candidates in their tracks: “Do you have any questions for me?” We’ve got a few suggestions for questions to ask at the end of your next interview, including: Is this a new position? If not, why did the previous employee leave? column1: The politics of the latest Trump indictment,"Plus: the rise and fall of ERIC and SCOTUS upholds VRA View this email online June 10, 2023 The Big Picture: Only in Trump’s America Charlie Neibergall/APThat a former president is facing federal charges would be a big deal unto itself. But add to that, these charges coming as said former president is running – and leading – in the primary to get his old job back, and you have something truly unusual and extraordinary. Only in Donald Trump’s America. Here are four ways to think about it all: 1. Not much has affected Trump’s trajectory to this point. It’s hard to see why this time would be any different – at least in the short term, say GOP strategists. 2. Trump has spent years, almost a decade now undermining the Justice Department and the FBI. That’s helped insulate him with his base, who don’t believe in these key institutions or the media. 3. Watch his Republican rivals. There is an appetite in the GOP for someone other than Trump, but most of his Republican rivals to this point have shown little interest in using Trump’s legal troubles to attack him. So far, they’ve instead attacked the DOJ. Does that continue and can they win without going after Trump on this and paint him as too weak of a candidate to take on Biden? 4. This 2024 election is going to be really weird. There’s never been a former president indicted and running for president again. Plus, if Trump gets through the primary, he’d face off with President Biden. Both are unpopular and would be the oldest candidates to run against each other. — Domenico Montanaro, NPR’s senior political editor/correspondentRead More Newsletter continues after sponsor message ICYMI: Top Stories Glenn Harvey for NPRThe rise and fall of ERIC: For years, the Electronic Registration Information Center, or ERIC, was an obscure, bipartisan system to share voter data across states and prevent fraud. Then 2020 happened, and Trump’s lies about the election mobilized conservatives against the existing election system. Now, driven by loyalty to Trump and far-right conspiracy theories, Republican states are abandoning one of the best tools available to prevent voter fraud. NPR’s Miles Parks spent months investigating
column2: you won’t want to miss this one. SCOTUS upholds VRA: The Supreme Court this week reaffirmed a provision of the Voting Rights Act that prohibits racial gerrymandering. The decision came as something of a surprise, given that Chief Justice John Roberts wrote the opinion that gutted the VRA in 2013. The ruling was a victory for Black voters in Alabama, but the decision could have major effects that reverberate across state lines. House grinds to a halt: Last week, House Speaker Kevin McCarthy scored a major victory by passing the deal he brokered with President Biden to lift the debt ceiling. This week, conservatives angry about that deal struck back, and brought all House activity to a stop until the GOP can regroup. If this sounds familiar, we predicted there might be trouble in last week’s newsletter. Now, it’s unclear what the conservative bloc of members wants or how long the stalemate will drag out. Biden vs. book bans: President Biden is tasking a point person at the Education Department to address the rise of book bans across the country. Challenges to books in libraries and public schools were up 40% from 2021 last year, according to the American Library Association. And most of the books challenged deal with issues of race and LGBTQ identity. The book ban coordinator is one of several initiatives around LGBTQ rights that Biden plans to announce at a Pride event this weekend. Speaking of classroom culture wars: Since 2021, Moms for Liberty has worked to fight COVID safety measures, limit discussions about race and LGBTQ identity in classrooms and elect fellow conservatives to local school boards. Now it's one of a dozen conservative ""parental rights"" groups deemed extremist by the Southern Poverty Law Center. — Lexie Schapitl, NPR Politics producer It all comes down to you Your financial support is the NPR Network's greatest strength. You keep the facts flowing. You bring more stories to more ears. You make a real difference when you contribute to independent, trustworthy media. Please donate today. Going Deeper: NPR Politics PodcastProsecutors say that the former president kept classified documents that he was no longer allowed to possess after leaving office and stored them in widely accessible areas in his Florida club. He is set to appear in a Miami courtroom on Tuesday. And: upcoming Supreme Court decisions and a send-off for Scott Detrow, who is a new host at NPR's All Things Considered. Listen to the episode. Mail attachements truncated,"Hi, I've got following problem. Sometimes mail attachments are truncated and and e-mail is send but files are corrupted. Mail header contains then: ""X-Amavis-Alert: BAD HEADER MIME error: error: part did not end with expected boundary"". And that is true - there is no boundary for MIME and data stream is not completed. I've turned on detailed postfix debugging and it looks like at some point there is: smtp_get: EOF instead of: < some_IP: QUIT > some_IP: 221 2.0.0 Bye Attachment is truncated after different sizes (sometimes 65kB, sometimes 200kB) and after different times (25s, 100s). The only common thing I've found is that all such mails come from one internet provider that is public-gprs*.centertel.pl I guess it might be the problem with this provider (Orange Poland) but on the other hand they can send e-mails through other mail servers without such a problem. I got stuck with it. Is there any way to debug it more? Do you have any ideas what could be the cause? I use postfix 2.3.8 on Debian Stable + amavis etc. Thanks in advance! Jakub",0,1

Last call: 9th CIVINEDU Conference 2025 🎉 organized by REDINE (Spain),"Dear colleague Helga, We hope this email finds you well. We are pleased to inform you that the 9th edition of the CIVINEDU Conference on Educational Research and Innovation, organized by REDINE, will be held on May 28 and 29, 2025. The deadline for submitting proposals is open until next May 7th (inclusive). • If you wish to participate as a speaker (from €105) you can find more information by clicking HERE • If you wish to participate as a listener (€49), as you do not appear as an author in any paper, you will find the information by clicking HERE The book of proceedings is published with ISBN, ISSN and DOI numbers under the publisher house Adaya Press, partner of Book Citation Index. Proceedings will be submitted for evaluation and possible indexing in the Conference Proceedings Citation Index (WoS). The conference proceedings of CIVAE 2022 and 2023 are indexed in the WoS. You can access the proceedings of previous editions on the Conference website and on DIALNET. For any questions, do not hesitate to contact us to: civinedu@gmail.com We appreciate all the diffusion you can make of the event. Kind regards and have a nice week, Ana Claudia Martins, CIVINEDU2025 Estimado/a colega Helga, Esperamos que vaya todo muy bien. Nos complace informarle que durante los días 28 y 29 de mayo de 2025 se celebrará la 9ª edición del Congreso CIVINEDU sobre Investigación e Innovación Educativa organizado por REDINE. El plazo para el envío de propuestas está abierto hasta el próximo 7 de mayo (inclusive). • Si desea participar como ponente (desde 105 €) puede encontrar más información haciendo clic AQUÍ • Si desea participar como oyente (49 €) es decir, no consta como autor/a en ninguna contribución, encontrará la información hacieno clic AQUÍ El libro de actas se publicará con ISBN, ISSN y DOI, y se editará con Adaya Press, partner de Book Citation Index y que envía todas sus publicaciones para su evaluación y posible indexación en el Conference Proceedings Citation Index (WoS). Las actas de CIVAE 2022 y 2023 se encuentran indexadas en la WoS. Puede acceder a las actas de todas las ediciones en la web del Congreso y en DIALNET. Para cualquier duda, estamos a su disposición en: civinedu@gmail.com Agradecemos toda la difusión que pueda hacer del evento. Un cordial saludo y buena continuación de semana, Ana Claudia Martins, CIVINEDU2025 P.S. The Big Quit Becomes The Big Stay | Gates’ Grad Advice | A New Future Of Work Newsletter,"Forbes Newsletters Plus: 3 Steps To Avoid Letting Data Overwhelm Your Decision-Making ADVERTISEMENT If there was any workplace phrase that defined my first year writing this newsletter, it was the “Great Resignation.” Until last fall, my coverage on this beat was defined by what companies were doing to retain their job-hopping workers, who, post-pandemic, were switching employers at record pace—or leaving employment entirely. I wrote about how companies were rolling out sabbaticals to help retain workers, mandating required vacation time and trying to lure employees with extra time off at the start of their tenures. The Great Resignation was a big cultural moment in the world of work, even if no one could really decide what to call it.What a change a year brings. Starting last fall, layoffs began mounting in the tech sector, and a shadow over the economy loomed, as more and more companies across industries froze hiring, slowed pay increases and sent some of those lofty perks—like extra mental health days or “laundry benefits”—by the wayside. Now, the Great Resignation may be giving way to the Great Resistance—to leaving jobs, that is—according to new data from ADP. In a post on ADP’s web site, ADP Chief Economist Nela Richardson said that in the first quarter of the year, voluntary job departures fell 5% compared with the same time last year, while the number of people returning to the job market has risen. Pay increases for job switchers peaked in June 2022 at 16.4%, but have been decelerating, with April pay gains for job switchers down to the lowest rate of growth since November 2021. “There are some signs that the ‘Big Quit’ of 2022 will lead to the ‘Big Stay’ of 2023,” Richardson said in the post, which Forbes’ Jack Kelly wrote about here. All too many people searching for a job these days are doing so not because they opted to leave—but because they got laid off. If that’s you, we have advice on how to answer key interview questions, negotiate your salary if you get an offer and write a powerful cover letter. If you’re planning to stay put and wait out this downturn before looking again, here are tips on developing the presence needed to advance, asking for help at work and promoting your side hustle.Starting next week, Emmy Lucas will be anchoring this newsletter, with a focus on news about jobs, career strategy and advice for early- and mid-career professionals. On Friday, I’ll be switching gears to launch our new Future of Work newsletter, which will be aimed at chief human resources officers, talent executives and other senior leaders focused on the trends, technologies and ideas shaping where the workplace is headed next. It’s part of a series of C-Suite newsletters we’ll be adding in the coming months, and you can sign up to receive the first edition here.It’s been a great run leading this newsletter over the past year and a half, but as it goes with careers, I’m excited to launch something new. As always, thanks to Emmy Lucas for helping me curate this edition, and hope you’ll consider signing up for our new newsletter! Jena McGregor Senior Editor, Careers and Leadership Strategy Russell Westbrook Means Business The former NBA MVP isn’t waiting until retirement to start building an empire. With a team of billionaire advisors, the 14th-highest-paid athlete in the world is already scoring with his fashion brand, advertising agency and other lucrative investments. Next up? Forbes’ Matt Craig looks at Westbrook’s un-sexiest business venture yet. However, a recent Indeed survey shows 73% of women re-entering the workforce reported facing significant biases in the hiring process because of their status as a stay-at-home mother. “To reduce this bias against parents, employers must realize that people can be good parents and good employees,” writes senior contributor Kim Elsesser. Meanwhile, Caroline Ceniza-Levine provides five tips to rebuilding your career post-maternity leave.Twitter’s new CEO: Elon Musk tapped Linda Yaccarino, NBCUniversal’s former head of advertising and partnerships, as his successor as the social media platform’s CEO—a hire Kim Elsesser says may be a “glass cliff.” Forbes’ Robert Hart takes a look at her background.How AI will shape early career jobs: Contributor Mark Perna explores why artificial intelligence could open up new career opportunities for young people. Bill Gates’ advice to new grads: In his commencement speech at Northern Arizona University Saturday, Bill Gates—a college dropout—shares the five pieces of advice he wishes he never received.The latest layoffs: British telecommunications company Vodafone said it plans to slash 11,000 employees over the next three years, while LinkedIn announced it will cut 716 of its roughly 20,000 positions. In a Federal Reserve survey, banks showed concern over a slowing economy, senior contributor Jack Kelly reports, as chairman Jerome Powell warns of job cuts. column34: >There are nearly as many ways to construct a rabbet as there are applications for them. Whether using hand planes or handheld routers, a durable set of drawers begins with proper layout. Securely nestling a glass inlay in a coffee table requires deliberate scribing and precise router setup. Woodpeckers Rabbet Gauges eliminate the trial and error in rabbeting, whether you're just starting out or have laid out hundreds using only your finger as an edge guide.

column2: >
column16: >Trusted by furnituremakers for centuries for its strength and ease of construction, the rabbet joint is often one of the first joints a woodworker learns to make. It's also one that can quickly be taken for granted, as the joint is quite simple to make but even easier to forego in lieu of the weaker butt joint. Our latest OneTIME Tool makes laying out rabbets effortless. Each Rabbet Gauge is 4"
column17: in length and has four steps, allowing you to lay out rabbets in standard stock thicknesses from a subtle 1/16
